# GNSS Lab — MATLAB Plot Guide

> **Script:** `ProcessGnssMeasScript.m`  
> **Course:** Wireless Security — Politecnico di Torino  
> **Reference tasks:** T4 (own data collection), T5 (spoof position), T6 (spoof delay)

---

## Overview — figures generated by the script

| Figure | Name | Content |
|--------|------|---------|
| `h1` | Pseudoranges vs time | Raw pseudorange per satellite over time |
| `h2` | Pseudorange Rate | Doppler-derived range rate vs reported PRR |
| `h3` | C/N₀ | Carrier-to-noise ratio per satellite |
| `h4` | WLS PVT | Weighted Least Squares position solution (North/East map) |
| `h5` | PVT States | Position offset, clock bias, velocity over time |
| `h8` | Geoplot | Animated geographic map of estimated positions |

---

## h1 — Pseudoranges vs time

Each line represents one satellite (identified by its `Svid`).

**Normal behaviour (no spoofing):**
- Lines vary slowly and continuously — the satellite is moving relative to the receiver
- Variations are smooth, roughly linear or mildly curved
- Parallel lines with similar slopes indicate a healthy, consistent constellation

**With spoofing active:**
- At `t_start` seconds a **sudden discontinuous jump** appears in all (or most) pseudoranges simultaneously
- Some lines jump up (satellite appears farther in the fake position), others jump down
- This simultaneous multi-satellite jump is the most visible spoofing signature in this plot

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** Lines should be smooth. Note any gaps (satellite lost) or sudden steps (HW clock discontinuity from battery saving mode — not spoofing).
- **T5:** Identify and annotate the jump at `t_start`. Measure the approximate magnitude of the jump for one or two satellites.
- **T6:** With `spoof.delay` added, the jump magnitude changes proportionally to the delay (1 ms ≈ 300 km of extra apparent range).

---

## h2 — Pseudorange Rate

Two curves are shown per satellite:
- **Computed:** `diff(PR) / diff(t)` — numerical derivative of the measured pseudorange
- **Reported:** `PrrMps` — Doppler-based rate directly reported by the chipset

**Normal behaviour:**
- The two curves closely track each other — they should be nearly identical for a healthy signal
- Values oscillate around a stable mean (typically a few hundred m/s), reflecting satellite orbital motion
- Residual noise is acceptable

**With spoofing active:**
- At `t_start` the computed rate shows a large **impulse spike** caused by the instantaneous pseudorange jump
- The reported PRR is unaffected (it comes from Doppler, which the software spoofer does not alter)
- This **mismatch between the two curves** at `t_start` is a detection indicator

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** Both curves should match. A divergence could indicate a poor-quality satellite or clock jump.
- **T5:** Annotate the spike in the computed rate at `t_start`. The reported PRR does not show the same spike — this inconsistency is a spoofing detection strategy.
- **T6:** A larger `spoof.delay` produces a proportionally larger spike, since a bigger pseudorange jump is introduced over one epoch.

---

## h3 — C/N₀ (Carrier-to-Noise Ratio)

Each line is the C/N₀ in dB·Hz for one satellite over time.

**Interpretation of signal strength:**

| C/N₀ range | Signal quality | Typical cause |
|------------|---------------|---------------|
| > 38 dB·Hz | Strong | Open sky, high elevation |
| 28–38 dB·Hz | Medium | Partial obstruction, medium elevation |
| < 28 dB·Hz | Weak | Near-horizon satellite, heavy obstruction |

**Normal behaviour:**
- Lines are noisy but stable within a band — slight oscillation is normal
- Satellites near the horizon have consistently lower C/N₀
- Indoor or urban-canyon collection shows more satellites in the weak band

**With spoofing active (software simulation):**
- C/N₀ is **not altered** by the simulated spoofer — it is a mathematical manipulation of timestamps, not of the physical signal
- This is itself a **detection opportunity**: if position jumps but C/N₀ remains perfectly stable, the inconsistency is suspicious
- A real hardware spoofer, by contrast, would often produce artificially strong signals (C/N₀ spikes above normal values)

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** This is the primary plot for describing data collection conditions. Count visible satellites, identify their approximate C/N₀ levels, and relate them to the environment (open sky vs indoors vs urban).
- **T5/T6:** Note that C/N₀ does not change at `t_start` despite the position jump — and explain why this is a detection indicator.

---

## h4 — WLS PVT Solution (position map)

A 2D scatter plot in local North/East coordinates (in metres), centred on the median position.

**Elements of the plot:**
- **Blue dots:** individual WLS position estimates (one per epoch)
- **50% circle:** the circle containing the best 50% of estimates — smaller = more precise
- **Median crosshair:** the best single estimate of position (`llaMed`)
- **Green square** *(if `param.llaTrueDegDegM` is set):* true position — useful for computing actual error

**Normal behaviour (no spoofing):**
- All dots form a compact cluster around the median
- A 50% circle radius of ~10–30 m is typical for a smartphone in open sky
- The cluster grows larger in poor signal conditions (few satellites, high HDOP)

**With spoofing active:**
- After `t_start` the estimates **jump to a new cluster** at a different location
- The displacement corresponds to `spoof.position` relative to the real position
- Two separate clusters are visible — one real (pre-spoof), one fake (post-spoof)

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** Report the median position, the 50% circle radius, and relate the spread to your data collection conditions.
- **T5:** Measure the distance between the real and spoofed clusters using the North/East axis values. Compare to the expected displacement from `spoof.position`.
- **T6:** Check whether adding `spoof.delay` alone (without changing `spoof.position`) shifts the cluster — and explain why (delay adds a bias common to all pseudoranges, which the WLS clock state partially absorbs).

---

## h5 — PVT States

Three sub-plots showing the WLS estimated states over time.

### Sub-plot 1 — Position offset from medians [Lat, Lon, Alt]

Deviation of each epoch's position estimate from the median, in metres.

- **No spoofing:** small random fluctuations around zero — estimation noise
- **With spoofing:** at `t_start` a step offset appears, corresponding to the displacement to `spoof.position`
- Useful to see *when* the attack takes effect and whether the solver transitions cleanly

### Sub-plot 2 — Clock bias

The receiver clock bias, expressed in metres (bias × speed of light).

- **No spoofing — slow drift:** the phone's oscillator runs slightly off GPS time; the bias grows slowly and linearly. If battery saving mode causes HW clock discontinuities, you see sudden steps unrelated to spoofing.
- **With spoofing — large jump:** the WLS solver cannot reconcile the fake pseudoranges with the real satellite ephemerides. It absorbs the inconsistency into the clock bias state, producing a **large abrupt offset**. This is the most robust detection signature because it is hard for a simple spoofer to eliminate.
- **T6 — spoofing delay:** adding `spoof.delay` without changing position adds a pseudorange bias common to all satellites. The WLS clock state absorbs it as a clock offset — the estimated position changes little but the clock bias shifts significantly. This is the physical equivalent of the spoofer being at a distance `delay × c` from the victim.

### Sub-plot 3 — Velocity [North, East, Down]

Estimated receiver velocity in the NED frame.

- A static receiver shows velocity near zero with small oscillations (estimation noise — even a stationary user is "moving" in the eyes of a noisy solver)
- A moving receiver shows a clear velocity profile
- At `t_start` with spoofing active, an apparent velocity spike may appear as the solver transitions between the real and fake positions

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** Comment on the clock bias drift rate and whether any HW discontinuities are visible.
- **T5:** The clock bias jump at `t_start` is the key observation — measure its magnitude in metres.
- **T6:** Compare the clock bias shift with and without `spoof.delay`. Explain the relationship: `Δbias ≈ spoof.delay × c`.

---

## h8 — Geoplot (geographic map)

An animated geographic map showing the estimated positions epoch by epoch overlaid on a real map tile.

> **Note:** requires MATLAB's **Mapping Toolbox**. If unavailable, this figure will error — comment out the `h8` section in the script.

**Normal behaviour:**
- A static user produces a tight cluster of red dots at a single geographic location
- A moving user produces a trail of dots following the real path

**With spoofing active:**
- The most visually intuitive plot: the animated dot **physically jumps** on the map at `t_start`, teleporting from the real location to `spoof.position`
- The jump distance is immediately interpretable in geographic context

**What to comment in the report:**
- **T4:** Screenshot of your real position on the map — verify it matches your actual data collection location.
- **T5:** Screenshot showing both the real cluster and the spoofed cluster on the map. Describe the geographic distance and direction of the displacement.

---

## Detection strategies — what to look for

Based on the six plots, the following inconsistencies are observable signatures of the simulated spoofing attack:

| Observable | Normal | Under attack | Detection logic |
|------------|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| Pseudoranges (h1) | Smooth, continuous | Sudden simultaneous jump at `t_start` | Discontinuity impossible in real physics |
| PR Rate spike (h2) | Computed ≈ reported | Large spike in computed, none in reported | Doppler is independent of pseudorange timestamps |
| C/N₀ (h3) | Stable | **Unchanged** despite position jump | Real signal strength cannot jump instantly |
| Position cluster (h4) | Single tight cluster | Two clusters, jump at `t_start` | Position teleportation is physically impossible |
| Clock bias (h5) | Slow linear drift | Large abrupt step at `t_start` | WLS absorbs pseudorange inconsistency into clock state |
| Geoplot (h8) | Dot at real location | Dot jumps to fake location | Geographic discontinuity |

The **clock bias** (h5) and the **C/N₀ invariance** (h3) are the two most robust detection indicators because they are hardest for a simple software spoofer to fake simultaneously.

---

## Quick reference — which plots matter per task

| Task | Must-have plots | Key observation |
|------|----------------|-----------------|
| T4 — own data | h3, h4, h8 | Signal quality, position accuracy, data collection conditions |
| T5 — spoof position | h1, h4, h5, h8 | PR jump, cluster displacement, clock bias step, map jump |
| T6 — spoof delay | h2, h4, h5 | PRR spike, position shift (if any), clock bias offset vs delay value |
| All — detection | h3, h5, h2 | C/N₀ unchanged, clock bias jump, PRR mismatch |
